one_line_takeaway: In decentralized learning under label poisoning, the seemingly 'vulnerable' weighted-mean aggregator has a topology-independent learning-error bound while Byzantine-robust aggregators (TriMean, FABA, CC, CG, IOS, RFA, LFighter) have topology-dependent bounds, so the weighted mean can provably and empirically outperform robust aggregators when the regular subnetwork is disconnected, sparse with high local contamination, or when global contamination is below local contamination.
key_finding: In the i.i.d. data regime, all aggregators (including the weighted mean) perform comparably to the unattacked baseline. As heterogeneity increases (mild non-i.i.d., then non-i.i.d.), robust aggregators' accuracy degrades and, in the constructed adversarial regimes, falls below that of the plain weighted mean -- e.g., in the non-i.i.d. two-castle setting (scenario i, global<local contamination) the weighted mean 'maintains classification accuracy' while most robust aggregators 'suffer performance degradation due to increased heterogeneity'; in the sparse/high-local-contamination fan-graph setting (scenario iii) with non-i.i.d. CIFAR-100 data, 'sparse connectivity of regular network and high local contamination rate cause severe accuracy drops for TriMean, FABA and IOS' while 'the weighted mean consistently attains the best accuracy.' The motivating example (Fig. 1, softmax regression on non-i.i.d. MNIST, 10 agents, 1 poisoned via label flipping) shows the weighted mean staying robust on both a two-castle graph and a fan graph while 5 robust aggregators (TriMean, FABA, CC, CG, IOS) degrade substantially, with robust aggregators falling below the weighted mean specifically on the fan graph. LFighter -- designed specifically against label poisoning -- is reported to perform poorly when data is highly non-i.i.d. and the regular network is disconnected. Note: the paper reports these accuracy trends primarily via plotted curves (Figs. 1-9) rather than tabulated numeric values in the extracted text, aside from the topology/contamination-rate parameters themselves.
core_contribution: The paper gives the first theoretical analysis of the weighted-mean aggregator's robustness to label poisoning attacks in decentralized (peer-to-peer) learning, and directly compares it against (rho, M)-robust aggregators (TriMean, FABA, CC, CG, IOS, RFA, LFighter). It proves (Theorem 1) that the learning error of any (rho, M)-robust aggregator running decentralized gradient descent under label poisoning depends on the network topology, while (Theorem 2) the learning error of the weighted-mean aggregator with a doubly-stochastic mixing matrix is topology-independent. A further result (Theorem 3) shows that for majority-dominant (rho, M)-robust aggregators there exist adversarial local cost functions forcing an error lower bound that scales with the topology, formalizing why robust aggregators can be worse than the plain weighted mean. The paper identifies three concrete regimes in which the (normally considered 'vulnerable') weighted mean provably or empirically beats robust aggregators: (i) global contamination rate below the local (max per-agent) contamination rate, (ii) a disconnected regular-agent subnetwork, and (iii) a sparse regular subnetwork combined with a high local contamination rate.
threat_model: Label poisoning attacks in a decentralized (server-less, peer-to-peer) learning setting: a subset of agents are 'poisoned' and possess corrupted local labels (label-flipping, e.g. class b flipped to B-b or 9-b), but otherwise faithfully follow the decentralized gradient descent protocol (they do not send arbitrary/unbounded malicious model updates). The paper explicitly distinguishes this from the more general and more powerful model-poisoning/data-poisoning threat model in which poisoned agents can send arbitrarily corrupted models -- label poisoning is characterized as a bounded, 'far from worst-case' attack (the poisoned agents' induced gradient disturbance is assumed bounded, Assumption 4). Two contamination-rate notions are defined: global contamination rate (fraction of poisoned agents over the whole network) and local contamination rate (maximal fraction of poisoned neighbors for any regular agent) -- the paper's central finding is that the relationship between these two rates, and the connectivity of the regular-agent subgraph, determines which aggregator (robust vs. weighted mean) is actually more robust.
limitations: The analysis is scoped to label poisoning specifically -- a bounded/weak attack in which poisoned agents corrupt only their local labels and otherwise follow the protocol -- and explicitly excludes the more general (and more damaging) model-poisoning or arbitrary data-poisoning threat models where poisoned agents can send unboundedly corrupted models. The theoretical convergence results (Theorems 1-3) are derived for deterministic optimization with full local gradients; the paper's own Remark 2 states that extending the tight bounds to stochastic gradients requires variance-reduction techniques and is left to future work, even though the neural-network experiments use stochastic gradients. Experiments use small networks (W=10 agents, R=9 regular) and specific hand-picked topologies (two-castle, line, fan) chosen to illustrate the three theoretical regimes rather than a broad sweep of real-world network structures (the paper notes additional topology/size variations are deferred to supplementary material). The comparison is limited to a fixed set of robust aggregators (TriMean, FABA, CC, CG, IOS, RFA, LFighter); newer or differently-designed robust aggregators are not evaluated.

quantitative_results: Scenario (i): two-castle graph, global contamination rate delta = 1/10, local contamination rate delta_max = 1/9 (delta < delta_max)
Scenario (iii): fan graph with line-graph regular subnetwork, local contamination rate delta_max = 1/3
Network size: W = 10 total agents, R = 9 regular agents across main experiments
Step size gamma_k = 0.1/sqrt(k) for softmax regression (full local gradients)
Step size gamma_k = 0.03/sqrt(k) for neural network training (stochastic gradients, batch size 32)

Three data-heterogeneity partitions: i.i.d. (uniform random), mild non-i.i.d. (Dirichlet distribution, alpha=1), non-i.i.d. (one unique class per agent for MNIST motivating example / ten unique classes assigned per agent in main non-i.i.d. partition)
Motivating example: 10 agents, 1 poisoned agent, label flipping b -> 9-b, compared on two-castle vs. fan graph topologies
baselines_compared: WeiMean (weighted mean with Metropolis-Hastings mixing matrix) -- the paper's focal (non-robust-by-design) aggregator
